executive_summary: The Australian dollar trades at 0.6845 against the US dollar, down 4% from its March 11 high of 0.7127, as the Iran-US war's risk-off impulse overwhelms the carry advantage from Australia's now-highest-in-G10 policy rate. The RBA delivered back-to-back hikes in February and March — raising the Cash Rate to 4.10% with a 5-4 split — making it the only G10 central bank to tighten in 2026, while every other major central bank held during the March 16-19 super-cluster. AUD/USD has been caught in a tug of war: structurally supported by the widest positive AU-US rate differential in over a decade (+71bps at the 10Y), GDP growth of 2.6% (G10-leading), and PPP undervaluation of 5-16%, yet tactically pressured by VIX at 30.6, CFTC positioning at multi-year bullish extremes, DXY at 100.5, and oil at $107-112/bbl which hurts Australia as a net refined-fuel importer despite coal/LNG export gains. Our tactical stance is bearish with low conviction — the positioning unwind and risk-off flows dominate the near term — while our strategic stance is moderately bullish, anchored by the strongest rate differential story in G10, structural USD weakness once war premia fade, and PPP undervaluation that provides a floor.

spot.convention: Rising AUD/USD = AUD strengthening; AUD is the base currency
spot.primary_index: RBA TWI 64.3 — slightly below mid-range; underperforming AUD/USD due to EUR and GBP outperformance in risk-off

key_crosses.convention: Near 10-year highs; peaked 1.2139 on 18 Mar; driven by 185bp RBA-RBNZ differential
EUR strengthening as relative safe haven in risk-off
GBP outperforming on BoE unanimous hold and lower beta risk
Tracking AUD/USD weakness; China-proxy channel intact

1m.direction: Sharp reversal from mid-March highs on war-driven risk-off

3m.direction: Positive but surrendered half of Q1 gains in final 3 weeks

6m.direction: Structural uptrend intact despite March pullback

1y.direction: Strong annual performance driven by RBA hawkishness and broad USD weakness

bis_reer.interpretation: The AUD is significantly cheap in real trade-weighted terms — approximately 21% below the long-term average of 126.8, placing it near the bottom quartile of its historical distribution.

valuation.trade_weighted_assessment: The RBA TWI at 64.3 sits slightly below mid-range. The TWI has underperformed AUD/USD because AUD has weakened more broadly against EUR and GBP during the risk-off episode, reflecting the relative safe-haven status of these currencies.
valuation.what_is_priced_in: The market has partially priced the RBA's hawkish divergence but the pullback to 0.6845 from 0.7127 has pushed AUD back below several PPP fair-value estimates. The OECD PPP fair value of 0.719 implies 4.8% undervaluation, while the Big Mac Index suggests 2.5-16% undervaluation depending on the measure. This valuation gap is not yet actionable tactically due to the risk-off overlay but provides a structural floor for the strategic thesis.
valuation.actionability: The PPP undervaluation signal is actionable on a 1-6 month horizon as a mean-reversion anchor, but may deepen tactically if risk-off intensifies. The BIS REER at the 25th percentile suggests AUD is cheap enough to attract structural real-money buying on dips.

cftc.percentile_context: Combined net spec positioning at ~+93K contracts exceeds the historical reversal threshold of ±80K and sits at the 95th+ percentile of the 3-year range — a multi-year bullish extreme.

positioning_summary.other_positioning_signals: EWA (iShares Australia ETF) shows tactical inflows (+$55M 5-day) but structural outflows (-$262M 1Y, -$1.04B 3Y). Risk reversals recovering from early-March lows suggest put demand is not accelerating despite spot weakness. Japanese institutional investors bought ¥213.6B of Australian sovereign bonds in June 2025 but rising JGB yields (2.36%, highest since 1999) are increasing domestic competition.
positioning_summary.interpretation: This is a crowded long undergoing an active unwind. AUD/USD has fallen 4% from its March high but CFTC data (lagged to March 24) shows positions have NOT materially reduced. The remaining overhang creates ongoing vulnerability: if AUD/USD breaks below 0.6800, stop-loss cascades among leveraged longs could accelerate the decline. The unwind typically takes 4-6 weeks to normalise from extremes of this magnitude.
positioning_summary.contrarian_assessment: The contrarian case for being long AUD is weak tactically — extremes need a catalyst to reverse, and the current catalyst is bearish (war, risk-off, DXY strength). However, if the unwind overshoots into net short territory over the coming weeks, the contrarian signal would flip bullish — that's the strategic entry signal to watch for.

market_structure_vol_summary.trend_regime: Bearish correction within a structural uptrend. AUD/USD broke below the 200-day EMA on March 24, ending a 5-month streak above this level. Price below all major MAs (50d, 100d, 200d). Weekly and monthly signals remain Strong Buy — larger uptrend intact.

support.significance: 2024 yearly open — first major support; tested in late March
38.2% Fibonacci retracement of the 2025-2026 rally; major psychological level
2026 YTD low (January); structural invalidation level below this

resistance.significance: 2024 high — first resistance; former support now resistance
200-day EMA — critical reclaim level; signals correction ending if recovered
50-day EMA zone — confirmation of trend resumption if broken

200d.spot_vs: Below by ~178 pips (2.6%); broken on March 24

moving_averages.alignment: Bearishly stacked (50d > 100d > 200d > spot) — all MAs above spot, indicating CTA/systematic trend-following flows have likely shifted from long to neutral or short

volatility.rv_vs_iv: RV ~13.0% vs IV ~12.5% — realised volatility slightly exceeds implied, indicating the market is moving as much as options imply. No significant vol mispricing to exploit; spot directional trades are being fairly rewarded.
volatility.risk_reversal_25d_1m: Recovering from most negative levels since October (early March); puts were bid during the initial selloff but demand for downside protection has NOT accelerated as spot declined further — consistent with long liquidation driving the move rather than fresh bearish positioning
volatility.vol_regime: Elevated/trending — war-driven uncertainty maintaining above-average vol. Daily ranges of 80-120 pips. High-vol regime favours momentum over carry strategies.

market_structure_vol_summary.key_observation: The divergence between spot weakness (below 200d EMA, bearish MA configuration) and the lack of accelerating put demand suggests this is a positioning-driven correction, not a fundamental regime change. A catalyst for positioning relief (Iran ceasefire, strong Q1 CPI) could trigger a sharp reversal — the 178-pip gap from spot to the 200d EMA could close in 2-3 sessions given current volatility.

market_context.recent_regime: The AUD is in a risk-off correction within a structural uptrend. The dominant narrative shifted abruptly on February 28 when Operation Epic Fury began, transforming AUD from a G10 outperformer riding the RBA hawkish divergence story into a casualty of the global risk-off. AUD's high-beta risk-on character is fully operational: it has sold off alongside equities (S&P 500 -7% YTD), underperformed safe havens (CHF, JPY, USD), and decoupled from the rate differential story. The March 17 RBA hike was a sell-the-fact event — the market was already positioned for it — and AUD/USD has traded lower in 10 of the 14 sessions since.

peers.rate_path_next_6m: Hold; 1 cut to 3.4% by December (dot plot)
On hold; possible hike if oil-driven inflation persists
Hold; cuts postponed indefinitely by oil shock
Hold; cuts postponed by oil-driven inflation
Hold at 0%; prefers FX intervention over rate changes
Continued appreciation bias in S$NEER band

key_spreads.interpretation: Widest since 2014; strongly AUD-supportive for yield-seeking flows
Very wide; AUD highly attractive vs EUR on yield basis
Massive differential; supports AUD/JPY carry trade despite narrowing trend
Positive but narrow; modestly supports AUD/NZD
Near parity; neutral signal for AUD/GBP
Extreme spread; SNB at 0% makes CHF a natural carry funder for AUD
Moderate; both central banks hawkish; AUD has slight yield advantage
Very wide; strongly AUD-supportive vs CAD

sovereign_yields.curve_shape: AU 2s10s: +32bps (positively sloped). Growth and inflation expectations intact; no inversion signal. Yield curve steeper than most G10 peers (US 2s10s near flat).

tactical.analysis: The RBA's Cash Rate at 4.10% is the highest in G10, sitting +35-60bps above the Fed Funds rate, +210bps above the ECB, +335bps above the BoJ, and +185bps above both the RBNZ and BoC. This is an exceptional positioning: Australia is the only G10 economy actively tightening in 2026, while every other central bank held during the March 16-19 super-cluster amid Iran war uncertainty. The 5-4 March vote split was about timing, not direction — Governor Bullock confirmed all members agreed further tightening was needed. Markets price a ~70% probability of another 25bp hike on May 5, with the terminal rate expected near 4.85% by year-end — implying three more hikes. The AU-US 10Y spread at +71bps is the widest since 2014, providing a powerful yield-seeking flow incentive for real money allocators. However, the tactical limitation is clear: in the current risk-off environment, rate differentials are NOT driving AUD/USD — the correlation between the AU-US 2Y spread and AUD/USD has broken down since the war began. The rate advantage supports AUD structurally but is being overpowered by risk sentiment in the 2-4 week window. OIS markets imply the RBA cash rate at ~4.28% by mid-May and ~4.60-4.75% by September, a path significantly more hawkish than the February SoMP assumed.
key_data_points: RBA Cash Rate: 4.10% (highest in G10; +60bps above Fed midpoint of 3.625%)
OIS-implied terminal rate: ~4.85% by year-end (3 more hikes priced)
AU 2s10s slope: +32bps (positively sloped; no inversion signal)
March vote split: 5-4 (timing debate, not direction)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets have almost fully priced the May hike (~70%) and at least two of three further hikes expected by year-end. The edge is not in the next hike — it's in whether the RBA delivers more than the market prices. If oil persists above $100 and Q1 CPI prints above 4.0%, the market may need to price a fifth hike, which is not yet in the curve. Conversely, if an Iran ceasefire collapses oil to $70, at least 50bps of priced tightening would evaporate rapidly.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months, the policy divergence story strengthens further. While the Fed, ECB, BoE, and BoC are all frozen in a stagflationary crosswind — unable to cut because of war-driven inflation yet reluctant to hike because of growth fears — the RBA is actively tightening into robust growth of 2.6% and trimmed mean CPI at 3.3% that was already elevated before the oil shock. Westpac projects the Cash Rate will reach 4.85% by August, which would create a policy rate spread of +110-135bps over the Fed, +285bps over the ECB, and +385bps over the BoJ — the widest differential set for AUD in modern G10 history. The structural carry argument is compelling: at 4.85%, AUD becomes the premier carry destination for JPY, CHF, EUR, and SEK-funded carry trades. The key risk is if the Iran war triggers a G10-wide recession forcing the RBA to pause — but with GDP at 2.6% and unemployment at 4.3%, Australia has more growth buffer than any other G10 economy. Even if the war shaves 0.5pp off GDP, Australia would still be growing at ~2.1%, comfortably above most G10 peers. Deputy Governor Kent's March 26 speech noting that neutral rates may need to be reassessed higher is a further hawkish signal that the terminal rate may exceed current market pricing.
key_data_points: Projected terminal Cash Rate: ~4.85% by August 2026 (Westpac)
Implied AU-US spread at terminal: +110-135bps (policy rate)
RBA February SoMP trimmed mean peak: 3.7% mid-2026 (pre-war; likely revised higher in May)
GDP growth buffer: 2.6% vs G10 median of ~1.4%
Kent speech (26 Mar): implied upward reassessment of neutral rate
strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices ~4.85% terminal by year-end. The strategic edge is that the carry differential will be in the widest percentile of its historical distribution at a time when other G10 economies are stagnating, making AUD an exceptional yield destination once risk normalises. A terminal rate above 5.0% — possible if oil stays elevated — is not priced at all.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical confidence is lower (70 vs 80) because the rate advantage, while real, is being ignored by the market in the current risk-off. The rates story is not delivering AUD gains tactically. Strategically, confidence is higher because the rate differential is widening further with each RBA hike, and once risk normalises, carry flows will reassert.

carry_vs_peers.interpretation: Attractive carry but high vol (JPY volatility surged with BoJ hiking expectations) reduces risk-adjusted return
Highest carry-to-vol ratio in G10; cleanest carry expression — 410bps carry vs relatively modest vol
Moderate carry with moderate vol; viable but not compelling
Decent carry with low vol; AUD/NZD is a clean relative value expression
Good carry but SEK illiquidity in stress reduces attractiveness
Similar carry to NZD; commodity correlation reduces diversification benefit
Negligible carry; not attractive as a carry trade
Small positive carry for long AUD/USD; carry alone insufficient to drive the trade

carry_analysis.carry_regime: Carry is NOT working tactically. In the March risk-off, carry returns were sharply negative as AUD sold off against funding currencies (JPY gained, CHF gained). VIX at 30.6 makes carry strategies dangerous. Strategically, if the war de-escalates and VIX returns below 20, carry will reassert as the dominant AUD driver.
carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Against JPY at 335bps annualised carry, AUD earns ~26bps (0.26%) over 4 weeks. At AUD/JPY 109.27, AUD can depreciate only ~28 pips before carry is erased — a thin buffer given current daily ranges of 80-120 pips.

tactical.analysis: Australia's inflation and growth data are unambiguously supportive of AUD strength through the rate expectation channel — GDP at 2.6% YoY is the strongest in G10, trimmed mean CPI at 3.3% is well above the RBA's 2-3% target, and the monthly CPI indicator printed 3.7% in February. The labour market remains exceptionally tight: February employment surged +48,900 against expectations of +20,300, and the unemployment rate held at 4.3%. WPI wage growth at 3.4% exceeds headline CPI on a monthly basis but trails the quarterly measure, maintaining real-wage pressure that keeps the RBA hawkish. However, the February CPI data pre-dates the Iran war — March data will be the first to capture the oil shock. Business confidence turned negative in February (NAB: -1) for the first time since April 2025. Westpac consumer sentiment at 91.6 remains deeply pessimistic, with over 75% expecting further rate rises. The growth story is strong but the consumer is under duress — GDP of 2.6% is driven by government spending and net exports rather than household consumption.
key_data_points: NAB Business Confidence: -1 (Feb 2026; first negative since Apr 2025)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced the February inflation data and is focused on Q1 CPI (29 April). A print above 4.0% headline / 3.5% trimmed mean would lock in the May hike and potentially bring forward further tightening — partially priced but not fully (~70%). A downside surprise (CPI below 3.5%) would be materially AUD-negative.

strategic.analysis: The strategic inflation picture is more complex because the oil shock introduces a supply-side inflation impulse that could prove stagflationary. Governor Bullock warned sustained oil above $100/bbl could add 0.75-1.25pp to headline CPI, and Westpac modelling shows CPI could peak at 5.5% by mid-year. This creates a dilemma: higher inflation reinforces the RBA's hiking cycle (AUD-positive through rates), but simultaneously erodes purchasing power and risks a consumer-led slowdown (AUD-negative through growth). Australia imports ~90% of refined fuel and held only ~38 days of reserves at the onset of the war. The offsetting factor is that coal and LNG export revenues have surged — thermal coal +40% since Q3 2025, LNG +85% from pre-war levels. Per-capita GDP turned positive for the fourth consecutive quarter, ending the per-capita recession narrative. The RBA's May SoMP will be critical — if it revises trimmed mean forecasts above 4.0% while maintaining growth above 2.0%, it validates the strong-enough-to-absorb-hikes narrative.
key_data_points: Westpac CPI peak forecast: 5.5% by mid-2026 (3-month oil disruption scenario)
Bullock estimate: oil shock to add 0.75-1.25pp to CPI
strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices a stagflation-lite scenario: more RBA hikes with growth slowing to 1.5-2.0%. What is underpriced is the possibility that Australia's energy export surge offsets the import cost, making the net terms-of-trade impact less negative than for other net importers. If the net effect is less stagflationary for Australia than for peers, the relative growth advantage widens.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical confidence (65) is higher than strategic (60) because near-term data is unambiguously strong pre-war. Strategically, confidence drops because the oil shock's second-round effects (consumer spending contraction, margin compression) introduce genuine uncertainty about sustainability of 2%+ growth through Q3 2026.

tactical.analysis: The risk environment is decisively bearish for AUD over the next 2-4 weeks. VIX at 30.6 sits in the fear zone well above the 20 threshold. The S&P 500 has fallen 7% YTD with five consecutive weekly losses. US HY credit spreads widened to 470bps mid-March (from 284bps pre-war) before partially recovering to ~321bps. AUD's risk beta is among the highest in G10: the rolling correlation between AUD/USD and S&P 500 is firmly intact at +0.3 to +0.5. AUD/JPY at 109.27 below its 50-day SMA confirms carry trade unwinding. Copper at $12,195/T down 15% from January peak reinforces the bearish signal. Moody's recession probability at 49% pre-war and BofA stress index at 0.79 approaching the 0.89 crisis threshold underscore severity. The April 6 strike pause expiry creates a near-term binary risk event that could trigger further risk-off if strikes resume.
key_data_points: US HY OAS: 321bps (up from 284bps pre-war; peaked 470bps mid-March)
tactical.what_is_priced: The current VIX at ~30 implies daily S&P moves of ~1.9%. AUD's 4% decline reflects the current risk level but has NOT priced a further escalation scenario where the Strait closure extends and Brent exceeds $130 sustainably. If that materialises, AUD/USD could test 0.65-0.66, another 3-5% downside.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months, the risk picture is more balanced. The base case for the Iran conflict is a negotiated de-escalation: Trump has transmitted a 15-point peace plan, a pause on energy infrastructure strikes is in effect, and Iran's military capacity has been significantly degraded. If oil falls below $80 on de-escalation, VIX would likely return to 15-20, triggering a risk-on rebound acutely AUD-positive given the currency's high beta. However, prolonged conflict and global recession cannot be dismissed. The probability-weighted outcome is roughly neutral: 50% de-escalation (very AUD-positive) partially offset by 25% escalation (very AUD-negative) and 25% muddling through (mildly negative). The CSI 300's relative resilience (-3.5% YTD vs S&P -7%) suggests China risk appetite is less impaired, modestly supportive for AUD through the China-proxy channel. Super fund rebalancing flows add an asymmetric upside tailwind that activates only in the recovery scenario.
strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices prolonged uncertainty but not a full recession. Credit spreads at 321bps are stressed but well below crisis levels (500+). If a ceasefire materialises, VIX compression from 30 to sub-20 would trigger a broad risk-on rally, and AUD as the highest-beta G10 currency would capture a disproportionate share.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish (80%) vs strategic neutral (55%) reflects different time horizons' relationship with the war. Over 2-4 weeks, risk-off is entrenched and the April 6 pause expiry is more likely to trigger renewed uncertainty than resolution. Over 1-6 months, de-escalation becomes increasingly likely, balancing the probability-weighted outcome.

tactical.analysis: Speculative positioning is at multi-year bullish extremes and represents the single most acute tactical risk for AUD. CFTC data as of March 24 shows leveraged funds net long +49,145 contracts (9-year high in gross longs) and asset managers net long +43,926 contracts (record gross longs). Combined net spec positioning of approximately +93,000 contracts exceeds the historical reversal threshold of plus/minus 80,000. Total open interest at 257,694 indicates robust market engagement. The positioning became stretched during AUD's +15% rally from early 2025 lows. Now with the war triggering risk-off, the crowded long is unwinding — AUD/USD has fallen 4% — but CFTC data is lagged. ETF flows show tactical buying (+$55M 5-day) amid structural outflows (-$1.04B 3-year). The risk is that if AUD/USD breaks below 0.6800, stop-loss cascades among remaining leveraged longs could create a self-reinforcing selloff. Risk reversals recovering from lows suggest the move is long liquidation rather than new bearish positioning.
key_data_points: Leveraged funds net long: +49,145 contracts (9-year high in gross longs)
Asset managers net long: +43,926 contracts (record gross longs)
Combined net spec: ~+93,000 contracts (above ±80K reversal threshold)
March 24 snapshot lag: positions may have reduced further in final week
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has partially unwound (4% decline) but positioning has NOT normalised. The remaining overhang creates negative skew for the next 2-4 weeks. A full normalisation would see net specs return to 0-20K, implying significant further selling pressure.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months, the positioning signal becomes less directional. If the war de-escalates and risk normalises, the current unwind will complete — and the resulting clean positioning slate (near-neutral) would be a bullish setup for the next leg higher, as fresh longs rebuild from a low base. Historical analogues suggest AUD enters a 2-3 month range-trading phase after extreme unwinds before the next directional move. If risk-off persists, the unwind could overshoot into net short territory — creating a contrarian bullish signal. Japanese investor flows remain structurally positive: Bloomberg reported yen 213.6B of purchases of Australian sovereign bonds in June 2025. Super fund hedging flows are mechanically AUD-positive when equities recover — Citi estimates a 5% hedge ratio shift could push AUD/USD up by 11%. Australian Retirement Trust has been actively rebalancing during the war.
key_data_points: Historical positioning normalisation zone: net specs 0-20K (from +93K)
Japanese investor AGB purchase: ¥213.6B in June 2025
Citi estimate: 5% hedge ratio shift = +11% AUD/USD impact
strategic.what_is_priced: The positioning overhang is known — the 4% decline reflects partial pricing. What is not priced is the potential for the positioning reset to create a powerful launchpad if a bullish catalyst emerges. The strategic assessment is neutral because the direction depends on the exogenous catalyst.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish (positioning is an active headwind as the crowded long unwinds) vs strategic neutral (the positioning reset creates a clean setup, but direction depends on the catalyst). Confidence is moderate at both horizons because positioning is a timing factor, not a directional predictor alone.
factors.contrarian_analysis_tactical: The contrarian case for being long AUD into extreme positioning is weak tactically. Positioning extremes need a catalyst to reverse, and the current catalyst is bearish (war, risk-off, DXY strength). The closest historical analogue is Q4 2017 when AUD net longs peaked near +100K and AUD/USD peaked at 0.8135 before declining to 0.7500 over 3 months. Current conditions (VIX already elevated, war ongoing) suggest a more compressed unwind timeline. The contrarian signal activates when net specs cross into negative territory.
factors.contrarian_analysis_strategic: At the 1-6 month horizon, the contrarian case is stronger. If the positioning unwind overshoots into net short territory, a de-escalation catalyst would trigger a violent short squeeze on top of the fundamental bullish case. The 2020 analogue saw AUD net specs hit -60K during COVID, then AUD/USD rallied 25% over 9 months. Monitor weekly CFTC data for the crossover from net long to net short — that is the strategic contrarian entry signal.

tactical.analysis: Australia's external balance presents a genuinely mixed picture. The January trade surplus narrowed to A$2,631M (below expectations of A$3,800M) as exports fell 0.9% while imports rose 0.8%. The Q4 2025 current account swung to a deficit of A$21,093M. However, the Iran war has introduced a powerful offsetting dynamic: coal and LNG revenues have surged dramatically. Thermal coal is up ~40% from Q3 2025 lows, LNG has nearly doubled from ~$10.50 to $20.40/MMBtu. Australia is the world's largest LNG exporter and second-largest coal exporter. The net effect is uncertain because the energy import cost (oil at $107-112) partially offsets export gains. Australia imports ~90% of refined fuel. Iron ore at ~$106/T is stable but not surging. China's March NBS PMI at 50.4 beat expectations, though the input costs sub-index surged to 63.9. The AUD/iron ore correlation at 0.76 remains historically high but competing energy dynamics may temporarily weaken this linkage.
key_data_points: Trade surplus: A$2,631M (Jan 2026; below A$3,800M expected)
Iron ore 62% Fe: ~$106/T (stable; +7-10% from Feb trough)
China NBS Mfg PMI: 50.4 (Mar 2026; beat expectations)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market is pricing the negative side of the energy shock (import costs, consumer squeeze) more than the positive side (export revenue surge). If Q2 trade data shows a dramatic surplus expansion driven by coal/LNG revenues, it would force a re-rating of AUD's external position.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months, the external balance tilts bullish. The energy export revenue surge has a lagged but powerful effect: higher coal and LNG prices sustained for even one quarter would generate tens of billions of additional revenue, swinging the trade balance back into surplus. Australia is structurally a net commodity exporter — iron ore (~35%), coal (~15%), LNG (~10%) together represent over 60% of export revenue. The IMF estimates for every $10/bbl sustained oil increase, Australia's LNG/coal revenue rises A$8-12B while oil import costs rise A$3-5B — a net positive of A$3-7B per year. Terms of trade should improve materially in Q1-Q2 2026 data. China's 4.5-5.0% GDP target backed by stimulus provides a supportive demand backdrop. The key risk is if China's property drag intensifies. The Australia-China trade relationship has normalised though the BHP iron ore restriction escalation is a new friction.
key_data_points: Australia commodity export share: >60% of total exports
IMF estimate: $10/bbl oil increase = net A$3-7B annual benefit for Australia
China property investment: -17.2% (2025; drag narrowing per Goldman)
strategic.what_is_priced: The market has not yet priced the energy export revenue surge because Q2 trade data will not be available until June-July. This is an identifiable mispricing: current AUD weakness coexists with an improving external balance not visible in the latest data. Strategic investors who can look through the lagged data have an information advantage.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral (55%) because near-term data is mixed and energy dynamics are offsetting. Strategic bullish (60%) because the lagged export revenue surge should materially improve external balances in Q1-Q2 data, and this improvement is not yet reflected in the market price.

tactical.analysis: The RBA's communication has shifted decisively hawkish since the February hike, and the March decision amplified this signal. Governor Bullock's post-March press conference left no ambiguity: the 5-4 vote was about timing, not direction, and she stated the unemployment rate will probably have to rise to bring inflation back to target. The March 17 minutes reinforce the hawkish bias: the Board described the oil shock as a material adverse supply shock and agreed further tightening would likely be required in the near term. AG Kent's March 26 speech went further, suggesting the oil shock could both push neutral rates higher and necessitate a more restrictive stance. The market is responding: OIS pricing shifted ~35bps higher since the February SoMP. The RBA's credibility remains high — it followed through on February signals by delivering in March. The gap between CB guidance and market pricing is narrowing: the RBA guides toward 4.35% May and further increases while the market prices ~4.85% terminal.
key_data_points: Last RBA communication: March 17 meeting + minutes (released 31 Mar)
Key message: Further tightening likely required in the near term
Market-implied rate vs RBA guidance: ~4.85% terminal vs at least one more (minimum)
Next communication: May 5 SoMP (first post-war forecast revision)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced the RBA's hawkish pivot. What is not fully priced is the magnitude of the May SoMP forecast revision — if trimmed mean CPI is revised to 4.0%+ peak with growth above 2.0%, it validates the full terminal path and could add further tightening expectations.

strategic.analysis: The RBA's credibility is a significant AUD tailwind over the strategic horizon. Among G10 central banks, the RBA is uniquely positioned: the only one actively tightening, delivering on guidance, and with a clear reaction function (oil shock → higher inflation → more hikes). This predictability reduces uncertainty around the rate path, making the carry trade more attractive for systematic allocators. Contrast with the Fed (frozen in ambiguity), ECB (eight cuts then halt, now reconsidering tightening), or BoE (unanimous hold after previously cutting). The RBA's communication architecture provides multiple high-signal touchpoints. Governor Bullock has demonstrated willingness to take unpopular decisions and communicate clearly about trade-offs. The strategic risk is political pressure around the May 12 Federal Budget, but the RBA's operational independence was reinforced in the 2023 review with no signs of interference.
key_data_points: RBA events (next 6M): May 5 SoMP, Jun 16 decision, Aug 11 SoMP, Sep 29 decision
CB credibility: high (delivered on guidance; clear reaction function)
Comparison: Fed frozen, ECB confused, BoE reversed — RBA stands out as decisive
Kent March 26: implied neutral rate reassessment higher
strategic.what_is_priced: The market trusts RBA guidance — the narrow gap between pricing and signalled path confirms this. The strategic edge is a confidence multiplier: because the RBA is credible and predictable, the carry trade is more durable than for currencies where the central bank could surprise.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both stances align (bullish) but strategic confidence is higher (75 vs 70) because credibility compounds over time — each successful hike delivery reinforces market trust, making each subsequent carry day more certain.

tactical.analysis: The Iran-US war is the dominant event risk for all G10 currencies and is particularly negative for AUD through the risk-off channel. The conflict entered its fifth week on March 31, with the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed since March 4, disrupting approximately 20% of global crude supply. Brent peaked at $126 on March 15 before the IEA coordinated a 400M-barrel reserve release. Trump announced a 10-day pause on strikes against Iran's energy infrastructure through April 6 and transmitted a 15-point peace plan. Iran has not accepted terms. The tactical calendar is acute: strike pause expires April 6 (binary outcome), RBNZ meets April 8. The secondary theme is US tariff policy: 15% universal tariff under Section 122 directly impacts Australian exports. The fuel excise cut of 26.3 cents/litre signals government concern. For AUD, the war operates through three channels simultaneously: risk-off (dominant), oil import costs (negative), and coal/LNG exports (positive).
key_data_points: Iran-US war: Day 31; Strait of Hormuz closed since March 4
Brent crude: $107-112/bbl (down from $126 peak; up from $67 pre-war)
IEA strategic reserve release: 400 million barrels (largest ever)
tactical.what_is_priced: Oil at $107-112 and VIX at 30.6 price a prolonged but contained conflict. A ceasefire (Brent to $70-80; VIX to 15-20) would be very AUD-positive but is not priced. Escalation (Brent above $130; VIX above 40) is only partially priced.

strategic.analysis: The strategic event risk assessment is neutral because the war's trajectory is genuinely unknowable over 1-6 months. The base case for the conflict is a negotiated de-escalation within 3-6 months: Iran's military capacity has been significantly degraded, the US has achieved its primary objectives, and both sides face domestic pressure to end the conflict. Beyond the war, the landscape includes: US Section 122 tariff expiring around July-August, the Federal Budget on May 12, the National Defence Strategy in April, Fed Chair Powell's term ending May 15, and the USMCA joint review beginning July 1. The Australia-China trade relationship has normalised but BHP restrictions represent a new friction. The range of outcomes is too wide for a directional stance with conviction. The Australia-China trade normalisation, while positive, is tempered by China's March 2026 BHP iron ore restriction escalation — the third since September 2025 — which represents a new friction vector.
key_data_points: Iran-US peace plan: 15-point proposal via Pakistan (not accepted)
strategic.what_is_priced: Forward markets price oil at ~$95-100 at 6 months, implying gradual de-escalation. AUD/USD consensus (0.70 end-2026) also assumes de-escalation. Faster de-escalation is an AUD-positive surprise; extension beyond 6 months would downgrade the entire forecast set.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish (75%) because the war is actively depressing AUD and April 6 creates imminent binary risk. Strategic neutral (50%) because the war's resolution is unknowable and probability-weighted outcomes across scenarios net to approximately zero directional signal.

tactical.analysis: Australia's fiscal position remains among the strongest in G10 and is not generating a directional signal. The AAA/Aaa/AAA sovereign rating from all three agencies with stable outlooks places Australia in the top tier globally. Gross debt at ~35.5% of GDP and net debt at ~20.1% are low by G10 standards. The MYEFO showed an improved fiscal position: deficit narrowed to $36.8B from $42.1B forecast. The war has introduced modest fiscal risks: the fuel excise cut costs A$2-3B over 6 months, and defence spending pressure is mounting. Victoria's state debt remains a sub-sovereign concern at $133.2B but is not a tactical catalyst. Bond auctions continue to be well-received given the high yield environment. This factor is genuinely not material for AUD given its strong starting position. The AAA credit rating provides structural support that is unlikely to change within the tactical horizon, making this factor genuinely immaterial for near-term AUD direction.
tactical.what_is_priced: Australia's fiscal strength is priced into the AAA credit rating and tight sovereign spreads. No tactical mispricing exists.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months, the fiscal picture becomes marginally more interesting with the National Defence Strategy (April), 2026-27 Federal Budget (May 12), and potential war-driven fiscal pivot. The budget will be the first post-war and is expected to include defence spending uplift. The Lowy Institute argued 3.5% of GDP defence budget would require higher taxes or forgone services. Rating agencies are not signalling concern. Budget impact on AUD depends on whether markets interpret it as stimulatory or restrictive. Victoria debt is unlikely to escalate to crisis within 6 months. On balance, fiscal and sovereign risk remains genuinely immaterial for AUD given its strong starting position. The IMF's 2025 Article IV flagged Victoria's debt as a potential national risk, noting the federal government may eventually need to support highly indebted states — though this is a multi-year concern, not an immediate market catalyst. Interest expense on Victoria's debt is projected to rise from A$5.6B to A$9.3B by 2027-28, creating a slow-burning fiscal pressure that is monitored but not currently priced.
key_data_points: Defence spending target: 2.5-3.5% of GDP (up from 2.0%)
Gross debt peak: 37.0% of GDP by 2030-31
S&P last review: Q4 2025 (AAA stable affirmed)
Budget revenue benefit from oil shock: higher mining company tax
strategic.what_is_priced: AAA stability is fully priced. The Federal Budget may deliver a modest surprise but is unlikely to move the market materially.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons neutral with similar confidence (70 vs 65). Australia's fiscal position is a non-issue at current levels. Strategic confidence marginally lower only because the Federal Budget introduces a small event risk.

tactical.analysis: AUD/USD's market structure has deteriorated sharply since mid-March. Spot at 0.6845 is below all major moving averages: 50d EMA (~0.7080-0.7091), 100d EMA (~0.7000), and 200d EMA (~0.7023). This bearish MA configuration means CTA/systematic trend-following flows have likely shifted from long to neutral or short, removing a significant buying source. The break below the 200d EMA on March 24 ended a 5-month streak above this level and historically signals a deeper corrective phase. Key support is 0.6811 with 0.6700 below. IV remains elevated (70th-80th percentile of 1-year range). The risk reversal profile offers a nuance: despite the decline, put demand has not accelerated, suggesting long liquidation rather than fresh bearish positioning. AUD/NZD at 1.1952 confirms the RBA-RBNZ divergence is intact, even as AUD/USD weakens.
key_data_points: 2026 YTD range: 0.6678-0.7127; current at 37% into range
Risk reversal: recovering from early-March lows (puts not accelerating)
tactical.what_is_priced: The break below 200d EMA and bearish MA configuration price a correction continuation. What is not priced is a sharp recovery: the 178-pip gap from spot to the 200d EMA could close in 2-3 sessions given current volatility, creating a compressed spring on any positive catalyst.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, market structure is less directional. Weekly and monthly signals remain Strong Buy, indicating the larger uptrend from the 2022 lows (~0.6170) is intact. The March correction (4% from highs) is within the normal range of pullbacks in a structural bull market — past corrections of 8-12% in 2023-2024 did not break the longer-term uptrend. The key level to monitor is 0.6678 (January 2026 low): a close below invalidates the structural bull case. Volatility will likely remain elevated while the war persists, favouring momentum over carry strategies. AUD/NZD offers a cleaner structural picture for directional AUD views, less contaminated by war and USD noise. The critical structural invalidation level is 0.6678 — the January 2026 low and 2026 YTD trough. A weekly close below this level would signal a trend reversal from the 2022-2026 structural bull market into a new bear phase, fundamentally changing the analytical framework from correction-within-trend to trend reversal.
key_data_points: Weekly/Monthly trend: Strong Buy (structural uptrend intact)
Critical support: 0.6678 (2026 YTD low; invalidation level)
strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices a correction within a trend. The relative value opportunity in AUD/NZD appears underexploited relative to the directional AUD/USD trade.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish (65%) because MA configuration and CTA flows are active headwinds. Strategic neutral (50%) because the longer-term uptrend remains intact and the current correction is within historical norms.
factors.expression_assessment: Given the vol landscape, the fundamental AUD view should be expressed with these considerations: IV is elevated (70th-80th percentile), making options expensive — spot expression is more efficient for directional views, or consider selling vol to fund directional exposure. RV likely matches or slightly exceeds IV, indicating no significant vol mispricing — spot trades are fairly rewarded. The vol term structure is likely in backwardation due to the April 6 strike pause expiry and Q1 CPI on April 29, making near-dated options expensive relative to longer-dated — favour 3M+ tenors for any options positioning. AUD/NZD offers a superior risk-adjusted expression: lower vol (~10.5% vs AUD/USD ~12.5%), cleaner trend, driven by rate differential rather than war noise.

tactical.analysis: USD liquidity conditions are tightening and the DXY's safe-haven rally is a direct AUD headwind. DXY at 100.46-100.54 has rallied to its highest since May 2025, gaining 2.62% in March. The Fed balance sheet continues shrinking under QT at $6.7 trillion, down from $8.9T peak. SOFR at 3.63% is stable with no dislocations. No emergency facilities have been activated and swap lines show no significant drawdowns. BofA market stress index at 0.79 approaches the 0.89 crisis threshold. The dollar smile framework is instructive: USD is on the left side (strong in risk-off) — the most AUD-negative regime. AUD underperforms at both ends of the smile, only outperforming in the middle (goldilocks). Cross-currency basis swap exact levels are unavailable publicly, but structural AUD/USD basis characteristics are typically positive. Quarter-end seasonal widening may temporarily distort.
key_data_points: Fed balance sheet: $6.7T (QT ongoing; -$2.2T from peak)
Dollar smile position: left side (risk-off = USD strong = AUD weak)
tactical.what_is_priced: DXY at 100.5 prices a moderate safe-haven premium. If the war de-escalates, DXY could return to 95-97, implying ~3-5% USD weakness directly translating to AUD/USD upside. The dollar smile transition from left to middle is the single most important macro condition for the bullish AUD thesis.

strategic.analysis: The strategic USD picture is modestly bearish for AUD with declining conviction. Fed QT is projected to continue through H2 2026 reducing reserves by ~$60B/month. The reserve scarcity threshold is estimated at $3.0-3.2T (current ~$3.3T), implying QT may need to taper by Q4 — a mildly USD-negative inflection. The March dot plot projects one cut in 2026, keeping USD yields attractive globally. Powell's term ends May 15 and successor uncertainty could affect swap line willingness. The dollar smile at the strategic horizon is more favourable for AUD: de-escalation implies a shift from left (risk-off) toward middle (goldilocks), the AUD-optimal regime. Even if the US economy softens mildly, this would weaken the right side without triggering the left side. The risk is if recession materialises (Moody's 49% pre-war), pushing USD to the left side again.
key_data_points: Fed dot plot March 2026: one cut to 3.4% in 2026
strategic.what_is_priced: Forward markets price gradual DXY weakness — the most popular macro trade globally. The risk is the USD bear trade is itself crowded; if recession materialises, the unwind would create a DXY rally crushing AUD.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons bearish but for different reasons. Tactically (70%), DXY rally and QT are active headwinds. Strategically (55%), the bias is barely bearish — QT tapering and potential dollar smile transition could flip this to neutral if the war de-escalates. Declining confidence reflects widening outcome range.

weighted_score.interpretation: Essentially flat, marginally positive. The two highest-weighted factors (Policy at 0.24 and Inflation at 0.18) are both bullish, but five lower-weighted factors are bearish and collectively outweigh them in the current risk-off environment.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: Net score +0.031 maps to Conviction 2 (low). The marginal positive score would normally suggest neutral, but the preponderance of bearish signals across risk, positioning, market structure, USD liquidity, and event risk — all with high confidence — leads to a bearish override at low conviction.

tactical.rationale: The tactical AUD picture is one of the most conflicted setups in G10. The two dominant factors by weight — policy rates (0.24) and inflation/growth (0.18) — are unambiguously bullish: Australia has the highest policy rate in G10 (4.10%), the strongest growth (2.6%), and a clear hiking trajectory toward 4.85%. Under normal conditions this would produce a strong bullish call. But conditions are not normal. The Iran-US war has triggered a risk-off regime (VIX 30.6, S&P -7% YTD) where AUD's high-beta risk-on character overwhelms rate differentials — the AU-US 2Y spread has widened by ~50bps since February, yet AUD/USD has fallen 4%. This decoupling of rates and FX is the defining tactical feature. Positioning adds urgency: CFTC net longs at multi-year extremes (+93K combined) are actively unwinding, creating mechanical selling pressure. Market structure confirms: AUD/USD below all major MAs with CTA flows likely neutral-to-short and the 200-day EMA now resistance at 0.7023. USD liquidity is also a headwind — DXY at 100.5 on the left side of the dollar smile. The April 6 strike pause expiry is the next binary catalyst. Actionability is moderate: this is a respect-the-risk-off call, not a fundamental reassessment. Short AUD/USD or short AUD/JPY offers the cleanest tactical expression. The carry cost of being short AUD is manageable given the spot move potential. The risk/reward of the bearish tactical call is unfavourable at 0.53:1 — reflecting the asymmetric reversal risk from a ceasefire catalyst that could snap AUD/USD 200+ pips higher in a single session. This poor risk/reward is the primary reason for the low conviction (2/5) despite the preponderance of bearish signals. For risk management, any tactical short should be sized conservatively with a stop above the 200-day EMA at 0.7023.

g10_ranking.commentary: AUD's G10-leading rates and growth cannot overcome its high-beta character in a war-driven risk-off environment. Oil-positive currencies (NOK, CAD) are outperforming commodity peers, while safe havens (CHF, JPY, USD) capture the fear premium. AUD ranks mid-pack — better than growth-deficient currencies but worse than anything with safe-haven or oil-long exposure.

conditions: Iran strike pause extended but no ceasefire
base_case.path: Grind lower with occasional bounces on ceasefire headline fades; 200-pip range oscillation biased to the downside.

tactical.path_expectation: AUD/USD is likely to trade in a volatile 0.6700-0.6900 range over the next 2-4 weeks with a downside bias. The pattern is a grind-lower with headline-driven bounces: each ceasefire rumour triggers a 50-80 pip rally that fades within 24-48 hours as the structural positioning headwind resumes. The April 6 strike pause expiry is the next potential volatility spike — expect a 100-150 pip reaction in either direction. Below 0.6700, the market enters panic selling territory; above 0.6943 suggests the correction is ending. The most likely path is a sideways-to-lower drift within a 200-pip range, resolving with the Q1 CPI on April 29.

increase_to_5_if: Iran escalation confirmed with Strait permanently closed
S&P 500 enters bear market (-20% from peak)
decrease_to_3_if: CFTC data shows continued liquidation below +50K combined

risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: 0.53:1 — unfavourable; modest reward but significant reversal risk
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Negative asymmetry — the bearish tactical thesis offers modest reward but significant reversal risk if a ceasefire materialises. This is why conviction is low (2/5) despite the bearish lean.

weighted_score.interpretation: Moderately bullish. Four factors bullish (collectively 0.62 weight), four neutral (0.21), one bearish (0.12). The dominant drivers form a coherent thesis: highest rates, strongest growth, credible central bank, and improving external balance.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: Net score +0.372 maps to Conviction 3 (moderate). The score is clearly positive and driven by the highest-weighted factors aligning bullish, offset only by USD liquidity headwinds.

strategic.rationale: The strategic AUD thesis rests on a simple proposition: Australia has the strongest fundamental story in G10, and once the geopolitical fog clears, the market will reprice AUD to reflect this. The rate differential is the anchor — at 4.85% terminal versus Fed at 3.4% dots, ECB at 2.0%, and BoJ at 1.0%, the RBA will be running the widest positive rate premium in modern AUD history, a 200-400bps spread against most G10 wide enough to attract systematic carry flows even in modestly elevated vol environments. External balance is the second pillar: coal and LNG export revenue surges will materially improve the trade balance and current account in Q2 data, turning a weak spot into a strength. The May SoMP forecast revision will serve as a validation catalyst — if trimmed mean CPI is revised above 4.0% with growth above 2.0%, it cements the strong-enough-to-absorb-hikes narrative. RBA credibility enhances durability: each hike delivery reinforces market trust, making each carry day more certain. The key risk is USD liquidity: if US recession materialises and the dollar smile pushes DXY higher, the thesis breaks down — this is why Factor 10 (-0.066) is the largest offset. The probability-weighted assessment assigns 55% to base case de-escalation (AUD rallies to 0.71-0.73), 20% to upside (rapid de-escalation + China stimulus = 0.74-0.76), and 25% to downside (recession, sustained war = 0.62-0.66). The expected value is positive: +195 pips probability-weighted. Actionability is moderate-to-high: the 0.67-0.69 entry zone identified by the tactical-to-strategic transition provides a clear price level for initiating strategic longs, with the 200-day EMA reclaim at 0.7023 as the confirmation signal.

g10_ranking.commentary: At the strategic horizon, AUD's G10-leading rate trajectory and improving external balance propel it toward the top. Only NOK — with both oil-positive exposure and a hawkish central bank — consistently outranks AUD. USD's ranking depends on the recession binary; in the base case AUD beats USD too.

conditions: Iran conflict de-escalates by Q2 end
base_case.path: U-shaped recovery beginning May/June following Q1 CPI and SoMP; grinds higher through Q3 as carry flows reassert.

conditions: War escalation (Strait closure extends through Q3)

strategic.path_expectation: The path to 0.71-0.73 is a U-shaped recovery, not a V-shaped bounce. Phase 1 (April-May): continued range-trading at 0.67-0.70 as war overhang persists; Q1 CPI on April 29 validates the RBA path. Phase 2 (May-June): SoMP revision + potential ceasefire progress triggers initial recovery above 0.70 and reclaim of the 200-day EMA. Phase 3 (July-September): carry flows normalise as VIX declines, systematic/CTA funds re-enter on MA reclaim, and super fund rebalancing provides mechanical support. Expect 1-2% pullbacks on negative war headlines within the overall recovery path.

increase_to_5_if: China announces RRR cut + property support

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Mildly positive on a probability-weighted basis. The base case alone (55% × 355 pips = 195 pips expected) delivers sufficient compensation for downside risk. Adding upside tail (20% × 555 pips = 111 pips) further tilts the expected value positive.

consensus_divergence.consensus_view: Major bank median AUD/USD forecast is 0.70 by end-Q4 2026. The consensus is constructive, driven by RBA hawkishness, PPP undervaluation, and expected USD weakness. Consensus assumes a gradual, linear path toward 0.70.
consensus_divergence.our_view: We are more constructive strategically (base case 0.72 vs consensus 0.70) but more bearish tactically (expecting 0.67-0.69 in the next 2-4 weeks). This implies a more volatile path than consensus envisions — deeper near-term trough followed by a stronger recovery.
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: +200 pips strategic (0.72 vs 0.70); -100 to -150 pips tactical
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: Consensus underweights two factors: (1) the magnitude of the energy export revenue improvement in Q2 trade data, which will swing the current account narrative from deficit to improving and attract real-money reallocation flows; (2) the carry-to-vol ratio at 4.85% terminal, which at 0.34 for AUD/CHF represents the most attractive carry trade in G10, attracting systematic flows that consensus models may not capture. On the tactical side, consensus underweights the positioning overhang at multi-year extremes and the severity of the current risk-off episode.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: An additional +200 pips (0.72 vs 0.70) from current levels = 5.2% return vs 2.3% for consensus. The excess return is material for any AUD-weighted portfolio.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If consensus is right (0.70), the opportunity cost is holding for a larger move that delivers only 155 pips instead of 355 pips. The downside of being directionally wrong is the same as consensus.

cleanest_bullish_expression.rationale: Long AUD/NZD is driven by the 185bp RBA-RBNZ rate differential (widest since 2008), already at 10-year highs with a persistent uptrend. The pair is less contaminated by war/risk-off noise because both currencies share similar risk profiles. Target 1.22-1.25 over 6 months. Lower vol (~10.5%) vs AUD/USD (~12.5%) provides a superior risk-adjusted expression.

cleanest_bearish_expression.rationale: Short AUD/JPY captures both the risk-off sell signal (JPY strengthening in crisis) and the BoJ hiking catalyst (narrowing the AU-JP rate differential from the JPY side). Target 106-107 on further risk-off. AUD/JPY at 109.27 below its 50-day SMA confirms the bearish momentum.

relative_value_pairs.thesis: Highest conviction relative value trade: fundamental divergence (RBA hiking vs RBNZ hold) is widening, persistent trend behaviour confirmed at 10-year highs.
Attractive on ceasefire: both commodity currencies but AUD's diversified basket outperforms CAD's oil concentration when oil falls. USMCA review risk adds CAD headwind.
Highest carry in G10 (410bps) — cleanest carry expression if vol normalises. Higher risk tactically as CHF strengthens in crisis.

cross_horizon_consistency.summary: Stances diverge: tactical bearish vs strategic bullish. This is a sell now, buy later setup driven by a temporary risk-off episode distorting fundamental pricing.
cross_horizon_consistency.divergence_explanation: Tactically, the war-driven risk-off and positioning unwind are high-beta, fast-moving dynamics that dominate on a 2-4 week horizon. Strategically, the fundamental pillars — rates, growth, external balance, credibility — are structural and persistent forces that dominate on a 1-6 month horizon. The weighted score flips from +0.031 (flat) to +0.372 (moderately bullish) because strategic weights increase the importance of external balance (0.06→0.16) and guidance (0.07→0.12) while decreasing risk appetite (0.10→0.05) and positioning (0.10→0.06).
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: May-June 2026, triggered by Q1 CPI (April 29), May 5 RBA SoMP, and potential ceasefire progress
cross_horizon_consistency.catalyst_for_transition: Reclaim of 200-day EMA at 0.7023 — a close above this level would confirm the correction is ending and the strategic bullish thesis is reasserting
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Tactical underweight / strategic overweight. Wait for positioning unwind to complete (target 0.67-0.69 entry zone), then establish strategic longs for the 0.72 target. AUD/NZD offers the cleanest all-weather expression for those unwilling to time the AUD/USD inflection.

1m.annualised_pct: -0.5% (AUD appreciating in forwards due to positive carry)

edge.1m: -92 pips (forward implies 0.6842, we expect 0.6750 — spot lower than forward; no edge for longs)
edge.3m: +63 pips (forward implies 0.6837, we expect 0.6900 — modest bullish edge emerging)
edge.6m: +371 pips (forward implies 0.6829, we expect 0.7200 — large bullish edge; primary capture horizon)

forward_vs_spot.interpretation: AUD/USD forwards trade at a modest discount to spot (negative points) because RBA rates exceed Fed rates, providing positive carry for long positions. Our expected path diverges sharply: tactically below forwards at 1M, but well above at 6M. The 371-pip gap at 6M represents the quantifiable edge in the strategic bullish thesis — this is where the carry + mean-reversion + de-escalation catalyst converge.

key_catalysts.rationale: Trump's 10-day pause on strikes against Iran's energy infrastructure expires. Extension would be mildly positive (reduces tail risk); resumption would trigger renewed risk-off and oil spike. AUD/USD could move 100-150 pips in either direction.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — market prices continued conflict at reduced intensity; escalation underpriced

key_catalysts.rationale: RBNZ expected to hold at 2.25%, reinforcing the 185bp differential with the RBA. Dovish commentary would further support AUD/NZD upside toward the 1.2139 high.
First post-war labour data. If employment remains robust despite the oil shock, it validates the RBA's growth assessment and supports further hiking. A sharp deterioration would challenge the thesis.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Not priced — market has not adjusted unemployment expectations for war impact

key_catalysts.rationale: The most important near-term data release. First quarterly CPI to capture the oil shock. Headline expected above 4.0%; trimmed mean above 3.5%. Prints at or above these levels lock in the May RBA hike and validate the hiking cycle.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — hike ~70% priced; CPI above 4.5% would force additional tightening expectations

key_catalysts.rationale: Fed expected to hold at 3.50-3.75%. Not a dot plot meeting so limited impact unless balance-of-risks language shifts materially on the Iran war.
~70% probability of +25bp hike to 4.35%. The SoMP includes the first post-war forecast revision — likely upgrading CPI significantly. This is the expected inflection catalyst for AUD/USD recovery.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — hike mostly priced; SoMP forecast revision magnitude is not

key_catalysts.rationale: First post-war budget. Expected defence spending uplift, productivity measures, fuel excise cut continuation. Fiscally stimulatory budget supports growth but complicates inflation. Surprise fiscal tightening would be AUD-negative through growth channel.

Successor uncertainty could affect market confidence in Fed independence and swap line willingness. Hawkish successor = USD-positive (AUD-negative); dovish/political successor = USD-negative (AUD-positive).
Second consecutive hike would bring the Cash Rate to 4.60%, widest positive spread vs Fed since 2008. Market currently prices ~60% probability.

Updated dots will reveal if Fed has shifted toward more cuts (AUD-positive) or fewer (AUD-negative). Currently one cut in 2026. Shift to two cuts would narrow AU-US differential trajectory but potentially support risk appetite.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — market prices ~1.5 cuts vs 1 dot

key_catalysts.rationale: Canada faces bilateral trade uncertainty from the USMCA review. If CAD weakens on review concerns, AUD/CAD benefits as a relative value trade. No direct AUD impact.
If hiking cycle continues, this meeting could deliver the Cash Rate to 4.85% — projected terminal. The August SoMP would include H1 2026 data and revised forecasts. This is the peak-rate catalyst.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — terminal 4.85% is consensus but not certainty

key_catalysts.rationale: China is Australia's largest trading partner (~30-35% of exports). Strong GDP data supports iron ore demand and AUD through the China-proxy channel. March PMI at 50.4 suggests Q1 GDP may meet the 4.5-5.0% target.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — market expects 4.5-4.8%; beat above 5.0% would be positive surprise

invalidators.condition: Brent crude sustains above $130/bbl for more than 2 weeks
invalidators.mechanism: Sustained oil above this level would likely trigger a global recession, overwhelm Australia's energy export gains with import cost and demand destruction, and force the RBA to pause.

invalidators.condition: VIX sustains above 40 for more than 5 trading days
invalidators.mechanism: Full crisis-level risk aversion (comparable to March 2020), producing AUD drawdowns of 8-12% regardless of fundamentals due to high-beta character.

invalidators.condition: RBA pauses hiking cycle at May or June meeting
invalidators.mechanism: Would undermine the rate differential thesis and signal growth deterioration not yet priced. RBA credibility factor would flip from bullish to neutral.

invalidators.condition: AUD/USD closes below 0.6400 on a weekly basis
invalidators.mechanism: Break of the 2022-2026 structural uptrend signalling a fundamental regime change from bull market correction to bear market.

invalidators.condition: China GDP prints below 4.0% for Q1 or Q2 2026
invalidators.mechanism: Sub-4% China growth would crush iron ore demand, weaken the terms-of-trade improvement thesis, and trigger a China-proxy AUD selloff.

invalidators.condition: DXY breaks above 105 and holds for 1 week
invalidators.mechanism: Signals USD entering a strong structural rally (right side of dollar smile), the worst possible regime for AUD which underperforms at both extremes.

invalidators.condition: CFTC AUD net specs flip to net short below -30K contracts
invalidators.mechanism: Extreme short positioning historically precedes AUD recoveries — the 2020 analogue saw AUD rally 25% from similar extremes. Contrarian entry signal.

signposts.threshold_confirms: Sustains above $110/T trending toward $120 (validates China demand + ToT improvement)
Falls below 22 (risk normalisation enables carry reassertion)

signposts.threshold_invalidates: Remains above +80K while spot declining (capitulation risk)

signposts.threshold_confirms: Sustains above 50.5 (expansion consolidating; iron ore demand supported)
Falls below 98 (dollar smile shifting to goldilocks — AUD-optimal)
signposts.threshold_invalidates: Breaks above 103 (sustained USD strength; worst AUD regime)

factors_changed.reason: 200-day EMA broken on March 24; all MAs now above spot; CTA flows shifted from long to neutral/short

signposts_triggered.implication: Neutral signal — not confirming or invalidating

signposts_triggered.outcome: Rose from ~25 to 30.6 (approaching but not breaching 35 invalidation)
signposts_triggered.implication: Bearish signal intensifying but not at crisis threshold

signposts_triggered.outcome: Stabilised at $107-112 (IEA reserve release prevented escalation scenario)
signposts_triggered.implication: Contained — the $126 peak was not sustained

signposts_triggered.outcome: Remains at extremes (+93K combined); unwind is the dominant new development
signposts_triggered.implication: Active positioning headwind confirmed; unwind ongoing

conviction_change.explanation: Tactical conviction decreased from 3 to 2 as the positioning unwind and risk-off intensified — the war entered its fifth week and AUD/USD broke below the 200-day EMA. Strategic conviction remained at 3 because the fundamental thesis is intact: the RBA delivered the March hike, Kent reinforced the hawkish narrative, and the energy export revenue surge is proceeding as expected.

new_catalysts: April 6 Iran-US strike pause expiry (new binary event)
Kent March 26 speech implying neutral rate reassessment higher
March 30 fuel excise cut of 26.3 cents/litre
May 12 Federal Budget (first post-war fiscal event)

seasonality.pattern: Q1 commodity contract resets (Jan-Mar) typically provide a seasonal AUD bid; April-May historically shows mild AUD underperformance as Japan fiscal year-end repatriation settles; August earnings season hedging by Australian corporates creates a modest AUD bid
seasonality.relevance_window: April-August 2026 — the mild April-May underperformance pattern aligns with the tactical bearish thesis, while the August hedging bid aligns with the strategic recovery timeline
seasonality.historical_magnitude: Seasonal effects are typically 1-2% and are overwhelmed by the current macro dynamics (war, positioning unwind, rate differentials)
seasonality.current_alignment: Modestly aligned with the tactical-to-strategic transition: April-May seasonal weakness supports the bearish tactical call, while Q3 seasonal improvement supports the bullish strategic call

assumptions: Iran-US war base case: gradual de-escalation over Q2 2026 with ceasefire framework by June; Brent retreats to $80-90 by Q3
RBA reaction function: continues to prioritise inflation over growth, delivering at least 2 more hikes to 4.60% and likely 3 to 4.85% unless GDP falls below 1.5% or unemployment rises above 5.0%
CFTC positioning data lag: March 24 snapshot does not capture full March decline; leveraged funds estimated to have reduced 15-25% since snapshot; asset managers near snapshot levels
Cross-currency basis: exact levels unavailable from public sources; structural AUD/USD basis (typically positive) used as directional input; carry calculations use policy rate differentials as proxy
PPP estimates: cross-rate fair values for non-USD pairs triangulated from OECD data with 5-10% estimation error
Implied volatility: tenor-specific IV data behind paywalls; percentile estimates based on qualitative FX commentary from public sources
China property: Goldman Sachs estimate that property GDP drag narrows from 2pp to 0.5pp assumed for base case
